So I want to start off with the little experience that I had, little narrative about an experience. So I walk into a room where there's some people waiting for me to pitch and I open the door and I stride in and I know who's going to be in there. Three guys worth a hundred million dollars, two billionaires and there are three analysts. And what's interesting in this room is I know that there are three analysts worth more than ten million dollars each. So when I say high stakes, high pressure environment, learn this stuff in the crucible, so I walk in the room, stride through and I open my arms like this and I look around and I say, let me decide who I'm going to kick off the island first. And the one billionaire says, call security and get this guy out of here. Okay. Now he didn't say that. So they laugh and they came into my frame. I can do that. I don't recommend you do that on day one, but I can do these things and I'm saying these things in this way so you can understand the structure. But you don't have to be as aggressive as me. I'm aggressive because I've dealt with so many investors. And investors want economic concessions for their power. They want to pay the least and get the most. So investors are a little bit different from customers. This is why I'm so aggressive. You walk in and it's kill or be killed. They want economic concession. You might hear about venture capitalists. They're called vulture capitalists. Right? It's the business. It's the business. When they invest, eight out of ten deals die and they have to live on the two that work. And they go in and they want the most. They want the most amount of your stock for the least amount of their money at the worst turns for you, the best turns for them and lock you in for the longest period of time. So this is why I've learned to be aggressive and social. Right? But animals and humans that are aggressive physically and verbally don't last long. Right? In the modern world. So I've learned to be aggressive about frame control and I get it very, very quickly, but I give it back. It's not about being dominant. It's about being able to get frame control, to qualify yourself as a prize, to deal with the power frame. But once you get it, once you get it, it doesn't mean you keep it and you dominate and you be the alpha and you beat your chest and you declare that you're the winner. You give it back, you play. So I want to say two things quickly. If anybody has ever been to see improv, improv comedy, show of hands. Okay. This is great stuff. So let me tell you what's going on there because I've been to some of these sessions in Hollywood. They can't be that funny all the time. What they do is they find a game to play. They find a game to play. And once they find that game, they play it. Right? And so that's what you want to do with frame controls. You want to get frame control, but then you want to get in a game. These are social games. In fact, psychologists and psychiatrists call it game theory, how human beings interact. It's called game theory. They don't go type that in the internet and reading it. It's horrible stuff to read. Okay. Nobody, it's not interesting. It's very academic. But it's game theory because it's a game. So you want to find a game that people will play with you. You get frame control, but you give it back. So for example, and I wrote about this and I've spoke about it before, but this is the most fun one. When my partner, he's a very interesting guy, and he'll say, okay, I'll meet you at the office at 317 and I'll have 19 minutes. I've heard this before. Right? And so what I used to do is get there at 317 and be like, oh my God, I have 19 minutes to do everything work. But then I learned to say, oh, you have 19 minutes? That's funny because I only have 18. Right? And then he'll say, okay, well then I only have, I forgot. I only have 15. I say, that's funny because you reminded me that I forgot. I have about nine minutes today. John, I'm sorry. 317, I can't even do that math, to 325 maybe, 324. Right? And he'll say, okay, well I'll meet you there, but we'll just talk from our cars in the parking lot. Because I'm super busy today. So you want to get in a game and we do this all the time. Right? And what's really fun is when you learn frame control and the guy you know work with all the time knows frame control and then you can play this game. Right? And you're working time frames and power frames and you'll say, oh my God, you just worked an analyst frame, stacked on a prize frame with an intrigue frame on top of it. You know, it's like these kung fu movies. Oh! Crane style. Scorpion. Elephant. Right? So you learn this game of framing back and forth and it's fun. Where I want to go next. So you're going to find your own way to do this. You don't have to do it aggressive as I do it. And I've been doing it for many years and it's one of the main thing I think about from the waking moments in the day to the last moment since the sun goes down is how can I walk in a room, get frame control for myself, get all these people under control, get them wanting what I have but having fun at the same time, do it in a short period of time and people leaving and going, that was awesome. Now what you're seeing here from me, you know, I presented to a board of a semiconductor company much of the same things I talked to you about. And in terms of their product, after half an hour of describing it to them, they stood up and clapped. Now that's interesting because it's a board meeting and that's not typical behavior. You would stand them and clap and then entertainment. And never experienced that before. But now because I'm getting stronger and stuff I really care about frame control, not just getting it but giving it and finding a game and letting people know I'm the prize and they're the commodity but letting them play with that and have fun and letting people know I care more about having fun than I care about money. So now I'm hearing things like, who are you? Heard that a couple of times. Give a pitch, who are you? And I've heard, where did you come from? I came from Mars to pitch the earth. But when you get good at doing this, it is amazing because it's not, see in some ways it's unfortunate the word control and frame control is unfortunate because you can think it's about dominating or selling or overcoming rejection or getting. But it's really about giving, right? I mean imagine that you're giving someone a game to play in a business meeting that's fun in which they can get value for themselves. It's awesome and you'll get acknowledged for it. I want to say a couple of little things. Sometimes I imagine when I meet someone there's a pipe between us. It's a pipe between us and value flows between this pipe because human exchanges are value exchanges. In our house, in your house probably the same, you cook, other person does the dishes. You wash the cars, they walk the dog. I don't know what happens in your house but it should be something like that. Otherwise I don't want to come over. The social reciprocation. And so we try to extend that to business meetings and say, okay, I give you a little bit of information. Now you give me some interest or you give me some value and we think it's a reciprocal value exchange but it's not. It doesn't work that way in a business context. We have to give huge amounts of value and that's what frame control is good for. So if there's this translucent pipe between Pam and I, it's an awfully long pipe today, all I'm trying to do is shovel value into this pipe as much and as fast as possible. I'm not selling, I'm looking to deliver value. I'm not asking her questions because questions suck value. Whenever you find yourself asking a question to someone, you are sucking value back to that value pipe. You're not adding value into it. So shovel value into the blue plastic frogs and describe how they're made and why you should care and what kind of semiconductor they put up their rear to make them out the front come insect repellent rays and what's happening to oil and why it's fun to go to France and you should go there and see these things in action and value, value, value, value and I don't ask questions unless it's an imperative. Give value and eventually what will happen, the human condition, we do understand there's reciprocal relationship because in her house, in your house, you do. I go get the mail and the other person pays the bills. There are these reciprocal relationships but when you meet someone, they're just different. They're just loaded differently. You add tons of value and then eventually that reciprocal nature will kick in and she'll go, oh, I got to give some value back and she'll start contributing and no questions were asked. So another way you know you have frame control is when you make a statement and somebody falls into that statement and starts to engage with you. See questions force people to engage with you. I hear a lot of sales trainers say ask questions to get information. You think the person you're selling to is an idiot? They know why you're asking questions so you can get information so you can leverage that information and sell them. It's questions take value. So just don't stop asking questions because your businesses are going to go to zero. People are going to be mad at me. Just observe what happens when people ask you questions, how you feel inside and when you ask other people questions, what it does to the social environment and you will see statements that people join into create these amazing social exchanges. Questions are just forcing someone to interact with you. So these are just some general things about the subject. I think I should go next is so people don't start saying, you're an amazing theoretician. You should be a college professor or write a book or something. Well seriously my dad was a college professor and I have deep respect for that profession and he was an academic, he was a demographer and when I got asked to write a book, initially my reaction was I'm in business, I do investment banking, I am in the middle of large deals, I am flying to Geneva and Russia and to La Jolla and I'm doing deals, why would I ever want to mess with this but then something in my background and upbringing kicked in and I got into it and it was a great experience, I'd love to be able to share that stuff but what I do want to do is make this deeply felt, this material let you know it's real world too. So I thought I would give you a pitch of a live deal that I've raised $15 million for in the last two months and some of this money I raised from people don't speak English and to me that's meaningful, it's one of the first times I've done that. So you've got a pitch that's complicated, you're going to hear it in a moment, it's something very, very complicated, genetics, molecular diagnostics, barely understand what the name of it is, go pitch it to someone in a different country that doesn't speak English, have them write you a $5 million check, then you know you're the real deal. And flying home from Moscow, I was just shaking my head and saying I cannot believe that that was possible. It was very interesting. So I want to give you a live pitch so you can see the exact same thing that I've been out raising money with, then I'm going to deconstruct it a bit and show you the elements of it. Let me shake the etch-a-sketch upside down. So I want to tell you about something very interesting here and thank you for coming today. If you can just hold off telling me about yourselves, I'd like to tell you about our genetics company and then in context of our company, I'd like to hear about your firm and what kind of things you've done. And I know you did great things in the 70s and your firm did great things in the 80s and in 2000 you invested in those three healthcare companies that went public. And that's all very exciting, but this is very topical. And as you know, in any stock presentation, they have to disclose that past performance of the future. So I don't think it's a good idea for you guys to tell me all the great things you've been doing because it doesn't indicate what you will be doing. So let me tell you about my deal and then you reflect back to me about your firm in context of what we have. I can pitch this in a few minutes. We can go into some Q&A. We have a little less than an hour to be here and then unfortunately we have to leave to our next meeting. So let's try and maximize our time together and then at the end we can decide if we want to advance to the next step or if it's just, but you will be interested in it. So I'll tell you something. It is the dawn of molecular diagnostics. And what's interesting about this is molecular diagnostics is all about genetic mutation. So as I look over there and I see he is dark skinned and medium build. And over there I see somebody of fair skinned and slender build and much more height. And over here I see beautiful women who are both fair skinned and dark hair and I see blonde women who have a dark complexion. All this difference between us is good. What it is is genetic mutation. These differences between us are genetic mutation. And we would not be able to survive as a species, as a human race if there weren't these strong differences because as we intermix our genes we have healthy gene pools to mix from. And so mutation makes the difference between us. And although the word mutation you're not used to, mutation is good except for when genetic mutation moves two standard deviations out from the mean. When you have too much mutation then you get adverse health conditions and you get disease and serious stuff like diabetes and lung cancer. Cancer of the eyeball if there is such a thing. But you get really serious stuff. You get fibromyalgia and cystic fibrosis and very serious health conditions when genetic mutation moves out too far, unhealthy. Well today there's a technology that's changing everything. And this technology is molecular diagnostics. It's the ability to measure markers in the human DNA. And some markers indicate adverse mutation and you can know what disease you're at risk for. For example many of you may have heard of the BRCA genetic test that women can take and you should take it. So the BRCA test has a very, very high predictive rate of breast cancer. And in fact you'll see people who test positive for certain parts of the BRCA marker will get mastectomies because without any existing disease condition other than the genetic marker they'll take action now. It's so predictive of that disease. So genetic markers are predictive of disease conditions. So your ability to measure an individual genetic marker and know what disease it indicates is an incredible technology and it's changing the entire healthcare system. In fact if you haven't heard of this today I'll tell you there's almost 200 drugs that have FDA black box labels on it that say you should get the genetic companion test for this drug. Because the genetic companion test will tell you if you metabolize, if you're able to metabolize that drug. So over half of drug spending is wasted because you don't have the genetics to metabolize. And you know this when you go out drinking somebody goes oh I can only drink one drink I get crazy. You know and then you have other people say oh I can drink 50 of these and it doesn't affect me. That's genetics. You can't train yourself to drink. Your genetics affect how you metabolize drugs and what diseases you have the propensity to get. So it's very interesting if you go, if you have a car accident and you get back pain and you go to your doctor and he'll start saying okay I got to start cycling you on oxycontin and morphine and codeine and there's 238 opiates and we got to find what you can tolerate. It's miserable. What does that mean? It means what, which of those drugs for you you can metabolize and absorb and it takes away the pain but it doesn't talk you, turn you into a zombie of the apocalypse. You got to find which of those drugs you can metabolize because of your genetics. So a genetic test will tell you what drugs you can metabolize. What drugs you'll have an adverse health reaction to and what diseases could be on your genetic train tracks. So I want to tell you a little bit about our company in a second but let me summarize the value proposition of a genetic test. One, it will tell you what disease you are at risk for. Number two, once you discover what diseases you are likely to get, it will tell you what preventative measures will be most effective in avoiding that disease condition. And number three, if you already do have symptoms of a disease condition, what drugs, diets and treatments are most likely to be effective. And if there's a better value proposition than healthcare today, I don't know what it is. This is a massive industry that's growing very, very quickly. Now let me tell you about our company. Many of you will have taken a genetic test already. So if you're doing an IVF treatment or someone in your family is, they will do a genetic test to see if you have the recessive gene for cystic fibrosis, for lung cancer, for many different conditions that are recessive diseases you could pass on. And if you're getting pain medication, it's likely that you'll take a genetic test to see if you can metabolize. If you're taking a drug called warfarin or plavix, you'll take a genetic test to see if you're a metabolizer of it. But these genetic tests are done one at a time. They're called singletrate tests. The test for cystic fibrosis to see if you carry that recessive gene is $800 for two people. The test for breast cancer is $3,000. The test for plavix is $400. The test for lung cancer is $600. The test for diabetes for how you metabolize carbohydrates and proteins and fats, that's $1,200. If you took every test that's available, you could spend $500,000. Our company is called Acme Genomics. Acme Genomics. I'm trying to avoid giving you the real name. Acme Genomics has realized that the cost of doing a single genetic test is about the same as testing for 33,000 genetic markers. So we put it all on a single chip. We test for 33,000 genetic markers on a single chip across 1,000 health conditions, diseases, drug interactions, food metabolization traits. So for $350, a company can buy for their employees a genetic test that includes over $150,000 of tests that the cost would be a singletrate test on a single chip. And from that you can learn all about your inherited disease, what diseases you might pass on, what complex diseases are on your genetic train tracks, how you could prevent those diseases, how you metabolize food and what exercise benefit you the most across a thousand of these conditions for $350. And it can be life changing. The company has tested 350 of the biggest losers. Gatorade and Pepsi have contracted to test 2,000 of their performance athletes with this test. So that's the value proposition of a genetic test. Let me tell you a little bit about the company. The company did $10 million last year. They'll do $35 million this year. But unlike many companies, the $35 million that we'll do this year are under contract. Existing contracts with large companies you've heard about that are in the Fortune 50. And by the way, in those contracts for 2013 are worth more than $100 million of revenue. So we're here today raising a $20 million round of equity for the company at a $200 million valuation. We've talked to J.P. Morgan about going public in the beginning of 2014 and J.P. Morgan does 50% of all healthcare offerings. The company has 24 resellers across the world, 10 of the Fortune 500 are its customers in under contract, and it is the dominant product. It is the dominant product in this market. You have the financials there in front of you, and so as you can see with this kind of pace of growth, with these kinds of customer contracts, with this kind of value proposition in which there are no competitors that have a product that is within two years of this one, capital raising is not a problem for the company. What we're looking for is good partners, people who can promote the company, find new customers, bring it into new markets. And so what I'd like to hear from you now, beyond any kind of money that you can offer, because we're going to raise the money in the next 60 days, what I'd like to hear from you is what kind of partner you could be for the company, what you could bring beyond your money, and how you could help the company reach its goals and objectives and its mission, and most importantly, to get an exit strategy for all the investors in 2014 with the IPOs we talked about. So I'll turn the table over to you for a bit and hear about your firm in context of our deal. So that is a pitch, guys. That's a pitch that has raised money. And as you can see, I didn't really talk that much about most of the things that you would probably talk about, very, very little about the product, because investors and buyers are much smarter than you think. Once they understand the value proposition, you don't have to spend any more time on your product. What you have to spend time on is calibrating how you guys are a fit for each other and getting control of the frame. And so what I'd like to do is a little bit of a tear down on this deal. Let's advance this slide so you can kind of see what happened. So even from the first page of the deck, and this is something people would see before they even heard the pitch. From the first page, we try and make it novel and provocative and interesting. This already doesn't look like two guys and a dog are promoting it. This is very important. In every pitch, so I tried to give you guys the short version, but in every pitch, you have to orient people to your deal by telling them exactly where it is, how many employees you have, what your product is, who you're for, who you're not for. You have to give enough information so at the end of your pitch, they don't scratch your head and go, that sounds awesome, but what is it again? You have to orient people to what you have, and it's a deal. You have to orient people to your deal. For me, I may have to read this to you because the copy is small. Most people are trained to give product overviews or brochures or executive summaries or business plans or PowerPoint presentations, and all of those are the delivery of information. What I have learned to do is give people the big idea, which is a little bit of everything, and I'll read this to you. Today, a single new technology is changing the economics of every healthcare segment, genetic diagnostics. A genetic diagnostic is valuable for three reasons. First, it can predict your chance of getting a serious disease, including cancer, diabetes, or a heart condition. Second, it will help you prevent the disease that you are at risk for. Third, it will help you treat any disease condition that you may already have. How? A genetic test panel will indicate how you metabolize certain drugs, which is critical information for treating any disease condition. Today, doctors and patients need genetic tests to make major healthcare decisions. The company sells large corporate customers a comprehensive genetic test panel for about $350, a fraction of the cost equivalent test results. The genetic diagnostics, it tests for 33,000 markers that provide insight. Now, this says 200, but more than 1,000 disease risks, drug responses, and health conditions. That is the big idea. It's so important to give people not what your product is, not what you are, not what they can get, but the big idea. Some frame the big context. If you did this, if you provided this for people, you could improve lives, save lives, make a billion dollars. It's a big idea. It's a big idea. So to me, this is incredibly important in the pitch. Next, this is very interesting. So see here, this says product highlights, but I don't talk about the product. Okay? Sold in volume to large companies. Diagnostic quality DNA and reports, 80% gross margin. You see the product over there, I'm not describing it at all. So people get, once you describe the value proposition, they get what you have. You don't spend the whole time talking about the product. They get it. They're a lot smarter than you think, especially investors. But I bet it's the same for your buyers, too. You have to do a lot. You could do a lot less explaining about the product than you think. Think about yourself. I could just tell you, I make a product, so you heard about my blue plastic frogs, and they're very effective at what they do. But I also make this great thing. It's a squirrel nut scratcher. How much more explaining do I have to do? I could talk a little bit about the size and the cost, the delivery time. But people are much smarter. They can intuit once they understand a value proposition, and you name the product, and they generally know what it is. So you don't have to spend. And most people come to pitch me a deal. They spend 45 minutes describing their product, and I don't know anything about the deal. And I don't know anything about them. And I don't know anything about the relationship. And I'll give you an example. So you see this a lot. Hey, guys. Good to meet you. Oh, I see you up on the wall. Is that your son playing baseball? My son plays baseball. That's really cool. Oh, he's in Little League, yeah, in Little League, but when we moved from Georgia, oh, you guys used to live in Georgia? We used to live in Tallahassee. It was closed. Oh, Atlanta. I love Atlanta. Yeah, Atlanta Braves. We used to go to their games. Oh, you used to go to their games? Yeah, I love them. What is this? This is comparison of geography knowledge. This has nothing to do with pitching, right? Because it's not creating any tension. It's not creating any social environment. It's not frame control. It's just not knowing how to do that stuff. You find yourself doing that stuff. It's not knowing how to get frame control and hunting and pecking for some source of alignment, and it's not good and it wastes time. And if you believe it as I do, that the human attention span is 20 minutes. You believe the human attention span is 20 minutes, then you will not waste a second telling people that you go to Chicago in the summer and like to go to Springfield to swim in the lake. All right? It's not helpful. Oh, so we were deconstructing this pitch. Company overview, 2012 revenue, long term licensing revenue, customer pipeline of $500 million, the size of the market. I mean, you see how different this is as a company overview? I'm not telling them the details. They can intuit the details or the things they already know, where it is, where they told them how many employees it has, to the important things, where revenue will come from, what the sustainability of that revenue is, how much more revenue there is behind it. That's what makes a company. You can start to see this pitch. A financial plan. What's interesting about this financial plan, you've seen hundreds. I give almost no information. Revenue, gross margin, cost of goods, expenses, and net income. At this stage of the pitch, if I see a hundred deals, a hundred of them give me much more information than I can absorb about their financial plan, a very, very summarized financial plan. Where is the revenue coming from? An overview of the customer. Proof points. What's interesting here is proof points. We're not just saying it's a great company and that revenue is going to come from somewhere. We're giving proof points. I do it in this way, but you need in your own business to give proof points. A little bit more detail further on. Here's what I wanted to show this with you. I'm not sure if this is page 10, page 11, page 12. It's the first time I talk about the product. I think if there's one thing to remember here, people care about the deal and they care about the social relationship and they care about wanting it, but the detail of the products can come much later. The value proposition. I think I might just read this top because I like it a lot. The general population, disease, endangers, health conditions go undiagnosed, causing considerable suffering. When disease conditions are properly diagnosed, drug effectiveness is a further problem. 14 of the top 20 drugs have limited effectiveness for the general population. Plavix, Warfarin, Statens, Abacavir. Will any of these work for you? Maybe not. Most of the population doesn't have the necessary gene mutation to allow these drugs to be metabolized. One proposition, very, very clear, great clarity. A little bit more specifics about the product. How do we know that if I invest in this deal or I buy this product, I didn't just enter a roach motel. How do I know that this investment gets an exit? We do an exit analysis to let people know that once you own this product or you invest in this company, you can resell it or you're going to make your money back or you're going to be able to sell the company off and get your money back. An exit analysis and a very clear exit strategy. This is the breakdown of how you deliver a pitch. In addition to the spoken pitch I gave, this is the written pitch. This you have seen full cycle how you go out and raise millions of dollars. It can no different. You can copy this. I think we have most of this stuff on the website. You can copy it word for word. You can watch the pitch I gave. But this is as long as you prize, get frame control, give a great value proposition, do it in a short period of time, do it as clearly as I did it, do it with some intrigue, give a good written document. You will be raising money or getting bigger buyers or doing partnerships much faster and of larger size than you would have thought it would be.
